title: 2025/12/03 - MA (Part 4)
displayTitle: 2025-12-03 - Māori Affairs Committee - 2024/25 Annual review of Te Aratuku Whakaata Irirangi Māori (Māori Television Service) [Part 4]
topic: 2024/25 Annual review of Te Aratuku Whakaata Irirangi Māori (Māori Television Service)
description: Wednesday 3 December 2025 | Māori Affairs Committee 2024/25 Annual review of Te Aratuku Whakaata Irirangi Māori (Māori Television Service) Hearing of evidence Submitters list: Te Taura Whiri i te Reo Māori (Māori Language Commission) Professor Rawinia Higgins, Chair Ngahiwi Apanui-Barr, Chief Executive Ngapera Hoerara, Deputy Chief Executive Pirimia Burger, Deputy Chief Executive
descriptionSummary: 2024/25 Annual review of Te Aratuku Whakaata Irirangi Māori (Māori Television Service). Te Taura Whiri i te Reo Māori (Māori Language Commission). Professor Rawinia Higgins, Chair

chunks.text: I was disappointed that he had been invited.

Thank you very much for coming before us today. We really appreciate it. We're looking forward to having a conversation, uh, with you. Um, but as, uh, a start, we, we will allow you maybe, uh, three or four minutes to give us a little bit of a, an intro, uh, before we get into q and a. So

Thank you very much for the opportunity to come and, uh, present in front of you. Just if I, uh, take an opportunity to cover off a couple of highlights that we are proud of as an organization, um, of our achievements over the past year, uh, particularly around a lot of our engagement and partnerships with other organizations to help support our aspirations for, uh, to achieve the audacious goal of, you know, achieving a million speakers.

Um, we realized that that can't just be done by us alone, needs to be in partnership with others. And of course, you may remember, uh, last year with the, um, quick selling out of the warehouse merchandise. Um, and it's those kind of partnerships that helps to promote the brand, the brand of, of Toto, um, amongst other things, but also with companies such as Skyport, um, and doing the Olympics, creating language for the Olympics so that there's an engagement in current, uh, at the time.

Um, and I see is not here at the moment, but just acknowledge her and her support of, of that working alongside the Olympic team. Um, and then of course, we continue to have an increased number of, um, people sitting their licenses for, uh, interpreters and translators, uh, which we see as quite positive that people are getting more and more confident, uh, with Terre to be able to sit the license, um, and achieve that.

And, uh, lastly, just a couple of our, um, variant reports around our engagement and supporting organizations with the work of helping to coordinate the Mahi Corona. Um, and, you know, 97% of them who believe that we did have a positive impact on working alongside them to help support them with their language, uh, aspirations.

Um, and of course we continue to try and build relationships with lots and lots of people to, um, ultimately, uh, deliver on the, um, as the vision in the Wahi Corona, which is, which means to spread the language as far and wide as possible.

So, on that note, I just want to thank you all for, for this opportunity, Gilda. And just before I take the first question from my fellow members, uh, let's just acknowledge the, uh, the audit report and that very, very good. So well onto to the organization with that itself. Okay. So with that, I'll, I'll call for the first question for members of that one.

Sure. Yeah. I have, thank you for being here. And I happen to be the one of the er early beneficiaries of the, the t-shirts of Oh, good. Good on, I smoking a cup of tea downstairs and we on the t-shirts. Um, you were very candid in your opening statement in the report.

Um, you said, uh, Terre Maori flourish continues to flourish in the hearts, homes and hopes of New Zealanders. There's also been the subject of resistance debate and political posturing. And then you go under say, these tensions only reinforce what many already know.

Language revitalization is not linear. It must be protected and propelled every day, every word, every speaker, every action counts. I wonder if you can, um, expand on what you mean by the political posturing and how that undermines the objectives of revitalizing and expanding the use of Al Maori.

I think it would be, um, uh, obvious in some of the statements around removing bilingual signage, um, and, uh, not necessarily, um, uh, open support for teil. Hmm. But I, I just wanna take a step back from that and what's happening today and kind of frame this in.

For the last 52 years, Maori has always been, uh, a part of our resistance and a part of, uh, a struggle that we've had. Um, and language revitalization is a three generational, uh, effort. It's not a, uh, it takes one generation to lose the language.

It takes three generations to restore it. And consequently, within those three generations within our whanau, people are at different parts of the journey. And I think whilst, you know, removing bilingual signage, uh, is not helpful from a language planning perspective. I can talk about that for hours, but I don't have that much time.

Um, it hasn't stopped the enthusiasm of people to continue to support il. And I think that's the thing that I'm really heartened by. And it is part of the resilience of IL always kind of being in the space of having to find opportunities to flourish.

And I think what I'm parted by as the commissioner is that people still continue to turn up for tu mm-hmm. People are still celebrating it, uh, despite whatever people might say about Il Maori. So that's kind of where that is Follow up. Absolutely. Are you concerned, um, that there's still, for some in living memory when it was forbidden to speak to Al Maori in schools, it's not the 1860s, we're talking about the 1950s, and are you concerned about the, um, um, removal of a requirement for school boards to uphold

as a regression to the idea that, uh, not only but, but also al Maori and te Maori, uh, not part of a broader societal cultural obligation that the Crown has and in turn the education structures of the Crown? Um, I think, um, so yes. Um, but I think within the remit of, um, what our focus is on how we continue to advance il mm-hmm.

And how do we continue to promote IL uh, in all spaces, you know, supporting, um, you know, uh, whanau to see that IL and society is still valued. And that is hard. It is hard. Um, but I think that's where we are heartened by creating partnerships, not just with government agencies, but actually with private sector as you find private sector, um, do see the value of as part of their brand.

Um, and so working, you know, sometimes you have to work with the willing, uh, and those who are already on board with the copa. And so I, you know, within the resourcing that we have is trying to work alongside those who are prepared to do that. Got it. Very diplomatic. I, yeah, just, yeah, I mean, my, my, my experience, I've been absolutely delighted to see it in terms, I've got 17, no 20 schools now.

Actually, my lectures just grown in, and every one of those schools is, um, is offering terre Maori and placing a really emphasis on, on, on growing that. And, and so, um, it's, it's fantastic to see. Um, within that, could you speak to, um, your role alongside TPK in the, on the development of the next, um, mahi?

Um, well, one of the things that we, we believe is that the mahi doesn't need a lot of changing. What it does need is consultation with the various partners to get agreement on where, on how we should change it. And that was something that was pointed out, uh, in the inaugural validation of the, of the Mahi Corona that the agencies didn't feel that they were listened to, uh, and that things were done to them then rather than done with them.

So for us, it's really, really important, I think, to call the agencies together or their representatives and to say to them, okay, this is where we want to go. What do you think? Um, you know, and, and get that agreement? Because for me, you know, if you don't get the agreement, then you don't get the commitment. Mm-hmm. And the other thing is right, I, I can't underline kindness enough in terms of the language, um, because nobody wants to go through this life changing decision to learn a language and then to be told that's wrong all the time.

Right. What they wanted, what they want is to be, uh, corrected in a way that keeps their mind up. It doesn't feel, make them feel like they're done. Right. Yeah. Um, so we, you know, the days of us taking people to task in the press of mispronouncing names and stuff along Kong, more likely to ring them up and offer them some help. But that's got to be a key part of the approach around the implementation of the Mahi Corona so that people will continue to engage or feel included and feel safe to learn and make mistakes.

Because the most obvious, uh, you know, consequence of learning something new is making a mistake. You know? And, and there's an old saying that's how you get it right. By making mistakes. So, you know, that for us is a big thing, but we reckon we could get it done and three to six months at the most.

And I have the absolute, I have absolute confidence in my team. We're a little organization, but we get things done, and that's what we pride ourselves on. Just to add to that, and more specifically to your question, I think we have, um, uh, continued to ask the minister to be able to work alongside TPK, uh, to help co-design what that might look like.

Mm-hmm. Um, and so there has been a bit of to and throwing, if I'm being honest, right. Um, but we look forward to the opportunity to be able to, uh, throw ourselves into that because we do feel like we are able to, you know, provide and contribute to the Mahi Corona refresh, um, based on the work that we've done over the past few years.

So at this stage, you don't feel like you are, um, included in that, in that process to the point you'd Like to be? Well, we've had meetings with them Yeah. And we've gotten, um, to the point of, of putting down a plan. Yeah. Um, and that's also around the development of policy as well, which my board has asked for. Um, and I think given the, the, the job cuts that have happened at, we are probably ideally placed to provide them with that support.

Right. We also have a, a crack research team, which is, I mean, look, if you look at most of the research that's been done into Ted Maori over the past, um, seven or eight years, it's been commissioned by us. So look, it's really, those are the things we bring to the table. We've made that offer to, to, um, the, um, initial is that they'll work with us, uh, on the Mahi Corona refresh.

We, we just, um, hurry to get it done because it's now been almost two years, uh, since the Ma Corona expired. I have, have a question. If I have a question, I be you to it. Um, um, thank you for being with us. I, um, I wouldn't normally go so deep into this, but I was looking, I opened the questions, the answers to the questions that you sent, uh, through, and I was really interested in, um, the gender pay gap, um, answers, which has completely gone down a totally different rabbit hole right now, um, but reduced from 27% in 2022 to 19% in 2024,

which is a big change. And I understand hadn't quite been measured when, uh, you've done this one. Um, that is a big change. But, uh, you know, the national average is, well, the national number I think is 8% now. Uh, so do you have some plans, some further plans to, uh, work towards reducing that gap further? Uh, yeah, we do. Um, what we tend to have, um, I, I can remember when my current HR advisor started, she said to me, we've got a problem.

And I said, why? She said, we have a gender imbalance, 75% of our staff, female. Yes. Um, and, uh, and a lot. And I said, well, when 75% of male will come back to me. Right. Um, and, and the issue that we've trying to be, we've been working with is bringing, uh, um, our, our further up the pay scale.

Yes. Um, a lot of them are working in the admin and advisor and sort of advisor, senior advisor area, but now, um, two of the three dce are female. Mm-hmm. Um, and, um, yeah. And we, you know, we're working at, um, actively working on closing that pay gap, And it's not easy.

I understand, you know, a small organization relatively, you know, medium size, it's not easy, but I just making sure that they're still working on Yeah. When, when we look at it in terms of bans, there's not that there's nowhere near a 19%, um, gap, but when you look at it overall, you aggregate it all. There is. Yes. Okay. Thank you. Just to add to that, and that's probably, I mean, there's probably no correlation, but I think the majority of, of, um, people who are in language revitalization who are very passionate about language that are woman Mm.

And so ideally, you know, our workforce will become, you know, largely woman as Natalie's already pointed out. Mm. So I'm hopeful. Keep going, keep going. Um, colleagues, I have a few questions, but happy to start with the one then you just, you just stop me when you want.

Um, thanks again for being here. Uh, with respect to the, um, the T-shirts, um, in the first 15 minutes at the Warehouse, um, the first store sold out, um, with the funds that come from that, I'm sorry, I tried to see, but I couldn't find, does that, um, is that part of maybe, um, sustainability planning in terms of finances going forward?

Oh, there are two things. Um, the first thing was we couldn't, um, get the gear out to people. We are not a, a clothing manufacturer or a retailer. Uh, and look, you know, I used to give away hoodies and t-shirts to people, and they had got their Christmas at all come at once, you know? Mm-hmm. Um, and so I was thinking last year, um, that we needed to find a way to get them out there, um, that didn't take away from the resource, uh, you know, uh, available to our staff.

So, and, and the staff at resource as well. And, and that's when the email from, uh, uh, one of the staff at the warehouse arrived. So the idea behind it was to take that financial burden off us because we couldn't get them for anything less than $60 per hoodie, a lot of money. And for me, that was just unsustainable. The second, second thing was there was a demand.

People love them. They, they wanted their little ticky on their, on the front of their hoodie. So when the Warehouse opportunity popped up, um, we went with it because we weren't commissioning anybody or giving away any money. Um, and now, I think the first, in the first year that we ran that, this particular year anyway, um, I think all up was about 80,000 in royalties from I think the sale of about 70,000 or thereabout, um, you know, uh, items of clothing.

And our royalty, I think is about 6%. So there were two reasons behind it. One, they, they could retail them at $17, oh, sorry. Wholesale them at $17. We couldn't, ours was 60. Um, so first one was financial sustainability. Uh, but the main thing for us is just, um, getting, you know, that ticky out there and being seen because people love the logo.

They love that brand. Um, and the Warehouse can do what we can't. So. Okay. Thank you. Can I ask another question too? Can keep going. Do we find someone else? But, okay. Okay. Thank you. Um, I wanna ask about, um, last year, if you recall, professor, we discussed, uh, how sometimes there's discrepancies in the EO when it comes to our elderly, our queer, that there's certain new words that have to adapt.

Like, for example, I think an example was umbrella. There was no such thing as umbrella back in the day before we arrived. Um, and so some of our ua, there's a bit of a struggle point there. Do you recall, professor, we, we touched on that and I see with the, um, with the state of today or, um, Maori report that, um, the, the speaker growth is being led by our young people.

Mm-hmm. Um, our women and, um, Maori rural regions, interestingly enough. Um, so I guess in terms of that subject matter we discussed last year, has there been any fado about how to, like, is there anything in the plans to be done with respect to how do we bridge that gap between the elderly and them not really understanding some of this kind of, Um, neo Maori?

Um, it's always a challenge, right? Because I think the language, all languages evolve over time. And so keeping up with new language is always gonna be a challenge for everyone. It's kind of, so while we continue to develop new words, uh, and socialize them, uh, it is one of the, um, uh, what I find more recently is social media has helped to advance that a lot more.

Um, but I think that complaint of can, will always be fair. Um, and sometimes it's just not even understanding, um, you know, the English word at, at the same time. 'cause some of the more nuanced words that are being used. And then you have another side of that where you have like the likes of Scotty Morrison who will use traditional words and they don't understand those either.

Mm-hmm. So there's always kind of a, um, that's where is really important, you know, the more we use, the more we, we start to adapt. And we, and we, even in English, we pick up new words, uh, that are created. Um, but we see it in its context, and then we under, we kind of understand we don't, probably not the first hearing.

We kind of think, I think I understand what that word means. It's kind of a nice word. Maybe I'll look it up later, never look it up. And then you hear it again. Think, oh, yeah, okay, I think I really do get it this time. So it is a bit of a challenge. But I think, you know, from, I went to tu um, the ma language festival and I actually watched the, one of the best presenters that I saw there was the Y Maima Knitting Club, and they were a group of who came together and they knit, and they don't have the language, but they're learning with each other.

And I think, you know, they were, she was fantastic. She, like the queer talked about, you know, who she used to blame everybody else about not having il. Mm-hmm. And it was one of the most powerful presentations I've heard, and I've heard a lot of presentations about IL. And, you know, she just, she went all the way back to, shall I blame Captain Cook?

And then she does this wonderful 30 minute narrative around all the different people, right. To her parents. And then this whole sense of loss of language and just hearing young people today using the language and wanting, you know, this desire. And so this knitting club, they have come together to try and help each other just a little ua.

Um, you know, I think the youngest member is probably about 85. Um, and so I think, you know, there are some communities who are coming together and creating those opportunities. I think Teta, why, um, who I know you're gonna see next their role is around how do they nurture that, those opportunities as well.

So they're probably best placed to see how to support Omata. But I do want to acknowledge the Wildlife Fund Club. That was fantastic. Thank you, Jen. I'm probably gonna make it a bit more boring now. That's okay. The Missing Club. Um, very cool story though.

And yeah, sort of puts that hole into action across the generations, but I wanna go back to the fact that reading your annual report, you, your budget has not changed for, or your revenue has not changed for since 2019. Um, and you've implemented some cost saving measures.

Uh, and part of that is a user pays, and part of that is putting things online. I guess the question for me is, how do you make certain that these things are equitable in that, and that you've still got the same reach? Or are you monitoring that to ensure that you've still getting out there to where you need to get out there and it's not just the few that can access it?

Oh, absolutely. And it's always a challenge. Uh, and we, um, we understand the challenge, uh, and have to find our ways to cut our cloth. Um, and I think, you know, I'm really heartened by the team, um, and the opportunities to look for different ways to try and get that reach.

And so, um, our social media reach, um, in the past year had grown. Um, and, you know, that is a space that a lot of our people are spending a lot of time in, um, and taking different approaches where it's not just here's, um, uh, a notice.

I, I'm kind of, I always get excited by what is the new meme that they've put up from, you know, some, uh, I think the last one I read was the, um, one from Wicked, you know, all translated. And, and I just think that's cool because, you know, that's reaching, you know, our kids to be able to think, oh yes, wicked could be in te.

Um, and so I think, you know, it's kinda trying to, um, be more innovative with, um, the opportunities that we have to be able to spread il, um, and try and be a little bit more relevant to, to our audiences as well. Um, so it's not just very, here's some research, we did this research and here are the results and lots of New Zealanders, it's still loving IL and yeah, for us, and it's kind of how do we kind of connect on a different kind of level.

So I just want to acknowledge the comms team for the lots of money that they've done in that space. Um, but, you know, it is a challenge, but, um, I'm always heartened by the fact that we might pivot a little bit to try a different unity, but, uh, our, the response and engagement that we have with other people, uh, with partnerships as well, continuing to grow those partnerships to help leverage, uh, with other people, uh, is, uh, has helped us through that.

Um, yeah, ask, Um, and that's great. I, I love hearing that, I guess off the back of what Rena was saying, um, that there's the older generation as well who, and I know not a lot of them, some, some of them do get to social media, but there's a big contingent that doesn't don't as well.

So we're ensuring that, how we're ensuring that those people are seeing your messages, that the connections are there as well through the knitting club might be one way, but Well, for sure, I'm not sure that's all. But because of us though, um, I think, you know, within the whole of our role is to be able to try and coordinate so that everyone is playing their role and, you know, the different, um, agencies and entities that are responsible for different parts of that they are to lead out on that.

And particularly with community more specifically is, um, ours, our remit is largely around supporting government agencies to be able to be more responsive in Europe. And how do they look like they're engaging? Yeah. Probably didn't put that right. Sorry. I mean, do, do you monitor that they are getting to the different generations as well?

So are those groups getting to the, you know Yeah. Ensuring that, that they're keeping modern, but also getting to the different generations? No. Yeah. We, we do, um, an annual survey, um, which is, we commission varying to do that survey, uh, and we trust the results they give back to us because researchers, their, their prime, you know, um, activity, uh, and we look through the different age groups to see how we're going.

Um, I know that one of, um, one of my DCE has brought along the latest Ian survey to share with you guys if you want it after we finished. Um, but yeah, we, we, we tend to, um, to measure in terms of, you know, the, the new email subscribers and things like that. Well, we used to email subscribers and followers, but now we combine that because they're pretty much the same thing.

If you don't want to be bugged with spam email, you go and be a follower. And that's where everybody tends to go these days, right? But the important thing is looking at that, um, at that, at that particular survey to see how groups ethnicities, et cetera, responding to re and it is specifically targeted around attitudes towards Del Maori, not towards Maori language programming or whatever else.

It's Rio Maori. And, you know, our country is amazing. 74% of the respondents to that particular survey come back saying they have a positive attitude towards Rio and is an important, um, part of our national identity. So, um, yeah, we do monitor it, although not specifically for age groups, but we get that, that particular Yep.

Um, thank you. And I think we would love to see the results of the latest Yeah, yeah. Agree. Yeah. Um, it's either a sucked there or potentially a primary. Um, just when I look at the, uh, the Maori language report a couple months ago, which was so insightful, um, so public support for Maori is growing, which is awesome.

Um, increasing number of students are learning it. Um, and Maori speakers have reached their highest ever, but, um, use in the home is declining. Uh, obviously that's gonna be, you know, fair chunk of our next conversation. Um, but what to, what, um, how is that, that finding, um, from you guys informing, uh, your, your developing work plan?

I think, um, all of that kind of information helps to steer us on what we prioritize over the different kinds of COPA that we we deliver in the year. Mm-hmm. So, and you know, as, uh, budget's always really tight, so we have to make some calls on, on certain things. So, uh, it very much helps us to think about, uh, what are the things we want to prioritize.

Um, in that report we were, I was quite pleased to use it as my state of the nation, so new initiative to try and kind of, you know, outline, you know, here is, um, the latest results of research, um, to hopefully kind of raise critical awareness. So I think sometimes when people understand, um, what's happening, then it's always a, you know, it's kind of, it's very helpful to kind of keep the messages being pushed out.

So for you, some specific, Well, just what, what, yeah, yeah. What I'm, because this is our, um, my second I've just clicked over two years and then pieces is my second time going through this process. And now I think I've got my head around where all the different, um, language entities that we have. Um, and so if you include for ka Maori and, and then 2K selves and five who are all focused on, and I mean, I, it's, it's really positive that what we're seeing is a, is a, is an increasing, um, enthusiasm for the place of Taal Maori as part of new,

both the New Zealand as part of our identity. Um, but equally it's, it's concerning to me that that's not translating into people using it more in every day. And so what I've been looking for, um, across today, um, as we've been speaking with the various commissions with TPK yesterday is, um, is shifts in terms of strategy towards, um, what I don't want to get in terms of the focus, focus on the second one, because obviously the first one's going really well.

Yeah. I, I'll, I'll just respond to that one because, um, it, it's been a, a problem that we've been fighting with for a bit, right? I remember last year telling me the story about your own son. Yeah, Yeah. And it's, that's similar thing that, you know, could kids, um, Henry did a, a a a brilliant with, um, doctoral thesis, which made the dean's list about why could the kids only speak Maori at school and at the ma.

Yes. And it's because those are the two places that they believe that Del Maori is being valued at. Mm-hmm. And so for a lot of, um, you know, for a lot of young people, um, they don't see Del Maori in general society and they hear a lot of English. Um, and so that's where they go to, and that's, that's a natural thing. You speak the language that everybody else speaks or you aspire to do that.

Um, so even those children who have gone to Kura and who have been in total immersion all of their schooling lives, that's what's happening. They're being influenced once they move out. I've got, um, staff at work who I have to continually remind who affluent Maori, you are lucky you've speak Maori to me, right.

And we're supposed to be the Maori Language Commission. Yeah. Um, and, and so we've had to, even, even in my, that that's there, and I think that's what this, uh, what this report, this finding points out is that while we are doing a great job converting the nation, um, we're still not converting them enough Yeah. To actually use the language all the time or as much as they can.

And just on that, of the five principles of language revitalization use is always the hardest. And it's the hardest to kind of encourage, because largely what you have is you have, um, language bonding so you can bond with a person, whatever your first language is with that person is often the language you continue to use, even if you know they can speak another language.

Mm-hmm. So subconsciously, so even if your whanau, uh, you know, so I'll give you an example. One of my best friends, her and I went to university together and, uh, we met each other and we spoke English to each other. And as much as, you know, I have to consciously switch off my English brain to speak to her.

And so, you know, it's, the language bonding is a, is a, is a really strong thing. And it happens in families. Mm-hmm. Yeah. So, um, and that's where it's really hard to make the conversion because the switch out is, uh, you're having to retrain or undo the bond rebo with another language.

And so it is like, it's a real thing. It's not a can't solve that I can't go into your home and say, stop speaking English and it will happen. Um, so there are, there are a number of contributing factors, but definitely use has always been the hardest one. It's the, it's the biggest challenge of all the five, um, principles of language revitalation.

And so I've always encouraged by families, um, and iwi who have like really bonded their children into only speaking and you know, it's become their normalized language. Mm-hmm. Um, and you know, that takes a lot of effort. I totally get, you know, I understand.

Um, but even when people walk in, they still speak to the old because that language bond is so strong. And I think that's stuff that we need, need to continue to work on. That's Fascinating. Uh, Steve, yeah, I mean, I'm, it is fascinating and I, my heart sort of sunk when I read that same point as Greg pointed out, that it's, um, it's a concern, particularly we are considering important role of consideration transmission that it's declining use in home.

But I wonder there must be, you must know about, um, the patterns of adoption of indigenous language in other parts of the world mm-hmm. Where revitalization has, uh, succeeded or is succeeding. Will it be that those, um, kopa kids, when they have their own children, we'll speak to the Maori to them in the home, is that that's what's Happening. That's definitely the aspiration and definitely what we seen.

There's signs of that so that Yeah. Um, in a sense that language bonding that they, they, those kids have with their own parents may have been in English, but now they will, they will language bond with their children. And often it is when you have children that you are motivated even more to use il.

And so, you know, trying to make that language bond than and there is, is important. Yeah. So, so how, how much should we be that concerned about it if there's, if that's part of it's gonna, the process that and a generation will see it flipped the other way. Um, I'm looking forward to it. Yeah.
